content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 211–212 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_014_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_014_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### You Are God's Children — No Mourning Like the Nations (vv. 1–2)

**1** You are children of the LORD your God. Do not gash yourselves or shave the front of your head in mourning for the dead. **2** You are a people holy to the LORD your God. The LORD has chosen you out of all the peoples on earth to be his treasured possession.

---

### Animals You May Eat (vv. 3–8)

**3** Do not eat anything that is detestable. **4** These are the animals you may eat: the ox, the sheep, the goat, **5** the deer, the gazelle, the roebuck, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep. **6** You may eat any animal that has a divided hoof — split in two — and that also chews its cud. **7** However, among those that chew the cud or have a divided hoof, you must not eat the camel, the rabbit, or the hyrax. They chew the cud but do not have a divided hoof — they are unclean for you. **8** Nor may you eat the pig. It has a divided hoof but does not chew the cud — it is unclean for you. You must not eat the meat of these animals or touch their carcasses.

---

### Fish and Birds (vv. 9–20)

**9** Of everything that lives in the water, you may eat anything that has fins and scales. **10** But anything that lacks fins and scales you must not eat — it is unclean for you.

**11** You may eat any clean bird. **12** But these you must not eat: the eagle, the vulture, the black vulture, **13** the red kite, the black kite, any kind of falcon, **14** any kind of raven, **15** the horned owl, the screech owl, the seagull, any kind of hawk, **16** the little owl, the great owl, the white owl, **17** the desert owl, the osprey, the cormorant, **18** the stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat. **19** All winged swarming creatures are unclean for you — do not eat them. **20** But any winged creature that is clean you may eat.

---

### Do Not Boil a Kid in Its Mother's Milk (v. 21)

**21** Do not eat anything you find already dead. You may give it to the foreigner living in your towns, and he may eat it, or you may sell it to a foreigner — for you are a people holy to the LORD your God.

Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk.

---

### The Tithe: Bring Your Abundance to God (vv. 22–27)

**22** Set aside a tenth of everything your fields produce each year. **23** Eat the tithe of your grain, new wine, and olive oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks in the presence of the LORD your God at the place he will choose as a dwelling for his Name — so that you may learn to always fear the LORD your God. **24** But if the journey is too far for you — if you cannot carry your tithe there because the place the LORD your God chooses is too far away and because he has blessed you — **25** then exchange your tithe for silver, take the silver in hand, and go to the place the LORD your God will choose. **26** Use the silver to buy whatever you like: cattle, sheep, goats, wine, beer, or anything else you desire. Eat it there in the presence of the LORD your God and celebrate — you and your household together. **27** And do not neglect the Levites living in your towns, for they have no allotment or inheritance of their own.

---

### The Third-Year Tithe for the Vulnerable (vv. 28–29)

**28** At the end of every three years, bring out the full tithe from that year's produce and store it in your towns. **29** Then the Levites — who have no allotment or inheritance of their own — and the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows living in your towns will come and eat and be satisfied, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work you do.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**vv. 1–2 — "children of the LORD your God"**: Hebrew *banim atem la-YHWH Eloheichem*. PNEUMA places this identity declaration as the theological frame for the entire chapter. The dietary laws and tithes are not arbitrary — they flow from who you are. Identity precedes obligation.

**v. 1 — "gash yourselves or shave the front of your head"**: These were Canaanite mourning rites — cutting the body and shaving the forehead to honor the dead. The prohibition is not about grief itself but about imitating pagan rituals that implied the gods of the dead had power. God's children mourn differently because they live within a different story.

**v. 2 — "treasured possession"**: Hebrew *am segullah* — a people of special treasure. Dynamic: "treasured possession" — the image is of something uniquely valued, set apart, kept close. Israel is not merely chosen for utility but cherished.

**v. 3 — "detestable"**: Hebrew *to'evah* — something that provokes revulsion, morally or ritually. The dietary prohibitions are framed as a matter of taste befitting God's people, not merely as health code.

**v. 21 — "Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk"**: This brief prohibition — appearing three times in the Torah — became the basis for the entire Jewish system of separating meat and dairy. PNEUMA renders it simply and literally. Its exact rationale remains debated: it may prohibit a known Canaanite ritual, or it may be a prohibition on mixing life and death (milk = life-nourishment; slaughter = death).

**vv. 22–26 — "the tithe as feast"**: PNEUMA draws out the striking joy of this passage. The tithe is not primarily about giving something up — it is a commanded feast in God's presence. The LORD wants his people celebrating abundance with him. "Wine, beer, or anything else you desire" (v. 26) is an astonishing pastoral generosity.

**v. 26 — "beer"**: Hebrew *shekar* — strong drink, fermented grain beverage. PNEUMA chooses "beer" rather than the archaic "strong drink" or the vague "other fermented drink." The text is not cautious here — it is festive.

**v. 27 — "no allotment or inheritance"**: Levites received no tribal land. The tithe system was Israel's mechanism for funding its religious infrastructure through shared abundance — not taxation but communal celebration with intentional inclusion.

**vv. 28–29 — Third-year tithe**: Every third year the tithe stayed local to provide for the Levite, foreigner, orphan, and widow. Four categories of vulnerability — the structurally excluded — are given systemic provision. The chapter moves from feast with God (vv. 22–27) to feast for the neighbor (vv. 28–29). Worship and justice are not separated. — *PNEUMA*
